authorship_evidence: Created by Contrast Security OSS team, pre-LLM era project with consistent human commit history dating back to early development

evidence.description: User-controlled command passed directly to os.system(command) with no sanitization, enabling arbitrary OS command execution
User-controlled command passed to subprocess.Popen with shell=True, enabling arbitrary OS command execution
User input encoded to bytes and passed to pickle.load(), which can execute arbitrary code via crafted pickle payloads
User input encoded to bytes and passed to pickle.loads(), which can execute arbitrary code via crafted pickle payloads
User input passed to yaml.load() with yaml.UnsafeLoader, which can execute arbitrary Python objects via YAML deserialization
User input passed to yaml.load_all() with yaml.UnsafeLoader, which can execute arbitrary Python objects via YAML deserialization
User input interpolated into SQL query via Python string format: EXECUTE_QUERY_FMT.format(user_input) then passed to cursor.execute(sql)
User input interpolated into SQL query via Python string format: EXECUTEMANY_QUERY_FMT.format(user_input) then passed to cursor.executemany(sql, ...)
User input interpolated into SQL query via Python string format: EXECUTESCRIPT_QUERY_FMT.format(user_input) then passed to cursor.executescript(sql)
User-controlled file path passed directly to tarfile.TarFile.open(user_input), enabling arbitrary file access via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to tarfile.TarFile(user_input), enabling arbitrary file access via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to tarfile.open(user_input, mode='r:bz2'), enabling arbitrary file access via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to bz2.BZ2File(user_input), enabling arbitrary file read via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to bz2.open(user_input), enabling arbitrary file read via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to io.open(user_input), enabling arbitrary file read via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed directly to open(user_input), enabling arbitrary file read via path traversal
User-controlled file path passed to execfile(user_input) (PY2), enabling arbitrary file execution and code execution via path traversal
User-controlled URL passed to urllib.request.urlopen(user_input) via _urlopen helper, enabling SSRF to internal services
User-controlled URL string passed to urllib.request.urlopen(user_input) via _urlopen helper, enabling SSRF
User-controlled URL used to create Request(user_input) object then passed to urlopen(), enabling SSRF
User-controlled URL path passed to HTTPConnection.request('GET', user_input), enabling SSRF via arbitrary URL path
User-controlled value passed as HTTP method to HTTPConnection.request(user_input, '/'), enabling HTTP method tampering
User-controlled URL path passed to HTTPConnection.putrequest('GET', user_input), enabling SSRF via arbitrary URL path
User-controlled value passed as HTTP method to HTTPConnection.putrequest(user_input, '/'), enabling HTTP method tampering
User-controlled URL path passed to HTTPSConnection.request('GET', user_input), enabling SSRF via arbitrary URL path
User-controlled value passed as HTTP method to HTTPSConnection.request(user_input, '/'), enabling HTTP method tampering
User-controlled URL path passed to HTTPSConnection.putrequest('GET', user_input), enabling SSRF via arbitrary URL path
User-controlled value passed as HTTP method to HTTPSConnection.putrequest(user_input, '/'), enabling HTTP method tampering
User-controlled value passed as host to HTTPConnection(user_input), enabling SSRF by controlling the target host
User-controlled value passed as host to HTTPSConnection(user_input), enabling SSRF by controlling the target host
User-controlled code string passed directly to exec(code), enabling arbitrary Python code execution
User-controlled code string passed directly to eval(code), enabling arbitrary Python expression evaluation and code execution

User-controlled code string passed to compile(code, 'vulnpy.py', 'exec'), creating a code object that can be executed. While compile() alone doesn't execute, it's part of a dangerous code execution chain.
User-controlled XPath expression passed directly to lxml root.findall(user_input), enabling XPath injection to extract arbitrary XML data
User-controlled XPath expression passed directly to lxml root.findtext(user_input), enabling XPath injection to extract arbitrary XML data
User-controlled XPath expression passed to xml.etree.ElementTree node.findall(user_input), enabling XPath injection
User-controlled XML passed to lxml etree.fromstring(user_input) without disabling external entity resolution, enabling XXE attacks for file disclosure and SSRF
User-controlled XML passed to xml.dom.pulldom.parseString(user_input) without disabling external entity resolution, potentially enabling XXE attacks
User-controlled XML passed to xml.sax.parseString(user_input, ContentHandler()), potentially enabling XXE attacks depending on parser configuration

Use of weak MD5 hash algorithm via hashlib.md5(). MD5 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive operations.
Use of weak SHA1 hash algorithm via hashlib.sha1(). SHA1 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive operations.
Use of weak SHA1 hash algorithm via hashlib.new('SHA1'). SHA1 is cryptographically broken and should not be used for security-sensitive operations.
Use of Python's random.random() which uses Mersenne Twister PRNG, not suitable for cryptographic or security-sensitive purposes
Use of Python's random.randint() which uses Mersenne Twister PRNG, not suitable for cryptographic or security-sensitive purposes
Use of Python's random.randrange() which uses Mersenne Twister PRNG, not suitable for cryptographic or security-sensitive purposes
Use of Python's random.uniform() which uses Mersenne Twister PRNG, not suitable for cryptographic or security-sensitive purposes

User input matched against catastrophically backtracking compiled regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via pattern.match(), enabling ReDoS
User input matched against catastrophically backtracking regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via re.search(), enabling ReDoS
User input matched against catastrophically backtracking compiled regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via pattern.search(), enabling ReDoS

User input matched against catastrophically backtracking compiled regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via pattern.fullmatch(), enabling ReDoS

User input matched against catastrophically backtracking compiled regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via pattern.subn(), enabling ReDoS
User input matched against catastrophically backtracking regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via re.split(), enabling ReDoS
User input matched against catastrophically backtracking compiled regex pattern r'((a)+)+' via pattern.split(), enabling ReDoS
Cache-Control header set to 'public' without no-cache or no-store directives, allowing sensitive response content to be cached by intermediaries
X-XSS-Protection header explicitly set to '0', disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter
Strict-Transport-Security header set to max-age=0, effectively disabling HSTS and allowing downgrade to HTTP

User-controlled query parameter 'user_input' is concatenated directly into a shell command string (cmd = 'echo ' + user_input + ' this should be echoed') at line 39, then passed to os.system(cmd) at line 43. This is a separate code path from the trigger module cmdi entries — the apps/fastapi_app.py /cmdi endpoint has its own direct os.system call with no sanitization, enabling arbitrary OS command execution.

jinja2.Environment is created at line 11 without autoescape=True (autoescaping is disabled by default in jinja2). User input from request.args.get('user_input', '') flows directly into template.render(user_input=...) at lines 19-21 in the jinja_xss view. With autoescape disabled, any {{ user_input }} in the xss.html template will render unsanitized HTML, enabling reflected XSS.
NOT VULNERABLE: cursor.execute(SELECT_ALL) uses a constant string 'SELECT * FROM Character' defined at module level (line 3). No user input is interpolated into this query.

NOT VULNERABLE: The name from the URL path is validated against TRIGGER_MAP keys at line 81 ('if name not in TRIGGER_MAP: raise NotFound()') and then sanitized at line 84 to ensure it matches exactly. This prevents path traversal in template resolution.

NOT VULNERABLE: connection_class(TRUSTED_HOST) uses the constant 'example.com' defined at line 21. The host is hardcoded, not user-controlled. Only the URL path or HTTP method is user-controlled in this helper.
NOT VULNERABLE: tree.xpath('/planet[name[text()="Kepler"]]') uses a hardcoded XPath expression at module level. No user input is involved in this query.

NOT VULNERABLE: do_raw(user_input) accepts user input but returns an empty string. The actual XSS vulnerability is in the framework handler files that concatenate user_input into HTML, not in this trigger function.
NOT VULNERABLE: do_parameter_pollution(user_input) accepts user input but returns an empty string with no dangerous operations. The function is a stub with no actual vulnerability trigger.

NOT VULNERABLE in practice: While os.path.join(TEMPLATES_LOCATION, path) could enable path traversal if called with unsanitized input, all callers construct the path from code-controlled values (TRIGGER_MAP keys or 'home' literal with '.html' suffix). The WSGI app explicitly sanitizes the name against TRIGGER_MAP keys before calling get_template.

NOT VULNERABLE at lines 136-137: After initializing connection with user_input as host (which IS vulnerable, documented separately), the subsequent c.request(TRUSTED_METHOD, TRUSTED_URL) call uses hardcoded constants 'GET' and '/' — these specific parameters are not user-controlled.
NOT VULNERABLE: sqlite3.connect(':memory:') creates an in-memory database with a hardcoded connection string. No user input is involved in the database connection.
NOT VULNERABLE: db_connection.executescript() uses a hardcoded SQL script string to create and populate the test table. No user input is interpolated into this SQL.

NOT VULNERABLE: io.BytesIO(user_input.encode('utf-8')) on line 7 merely creates a bytes buffer from the user input. BytesIO itself does not deserialize or execute anything — the vulnerability is on the next line (pickle.load), not in the BytesIO call.

NOT VULNERABLE: import_module('vulnpy.trigger.{}'.format(name)) could look like dynamic code loading, but the name parameter comes from _get_trigger_files() which only returns filenames from the trigger directory, not user input.
NOT VULNERABLE: lxml.etree.parse(StringIO(xml)) parses a hardcoded XML string defined at module level (lines 5-17). No user input is involved in this XML parsing operation.

NOT VULNERABLE to XXE: ET.fromstring(xml) parses the hardcoded module-level xml variable. While the user controls the XPath expression passed to findall(), the XML content itself is not user-controlled, so XXE is not possible here.

NOT VULNERABLE: The _hash helper function simply calls hasher.update(b) and hasher.hexdigest(). The weakness is in the choice of hash algorithm (MD5/SHA1) made by the caller, not in the update/hexdigest operations themselves.

NOT VULNERABLE at this line: PATTERN = r'((a)+)+' is just a string constant definition. The vulnerability only materializes when this pattern is used with user-controlled input in the re.* functions below. The pattern definition itself is not a vulnerability.
